symptoms.symptom_name: Acutely tender focal nodule at the eyelid margin with a pointing pustule at a lash follicle
symptoms.character: tender, focal, with a yellow pointing pustule

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A tender lid-margin nodule with a pointing pustule at a lash follicle is the defining feature of an external hordeolum.
A chalazion is characteristically painless, chronic and deeper in the tarsal plate — acute tenderness with a pointing pustule argues against it.
Mechanical irritation from the lid-margin lesion produces grittiness and reflex tearing.
Tearing and grittiness are non-specific and do not discriminate a hordeolum from mild conjunctival irritation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Erythema and oedema confined to the lid margin (not diffuse periorbital involvement)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Focal lid-margin inflammation localises the process to a lash gland rather than the soft tissues.
Preseptal cellulitis is defined by diffuse eyelid/periorbital erythema and oedema; its absence (focal redness only) is required to safely exclude it.
Diffuse periorbital spread would raise concern for orbital involvement; confined lid-margin redness argues against it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal visual acuity (no blurring or vision loss)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reduced visual acuity is a cardinal sign of orbital/optic-nerve involvement; normal vision is required to exclude orbital cellulitis on history.

symptoms.symptom_name: No painful or restricted eye movements and no diplopia

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Painful/restricted eye movements and diplopia indicate extraocular muscle involvement in orbital cellulitis; their absence is required to exclude it.
Fever and systemic upset accompany spreading cellulitis; being afebrile and well argues against significant soft-tissue infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: Full painless extraocular movements, no proptosis, normal pupillary reflexes and grossly intact acuity on formal examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Clinician-verified normal ocular motility, absence of proptosis and normal pupils definitively exclude orbital involvement — a sealed examination the AI Doctor cannot perform remotely; it must instead rely on patient-reportable proxies and safety-net.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal, uninflamed bulbar and palpebral conjunctiva on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A normal, uninflamed conjunctiva on examination excludes diffuse conjunctivitis as the cause of the redness and watering.
Meibomian gland dysfunction predisposes to lid-margin gland blockage and recurrent hordeola and chalazia.
The same gland dysfunction predisposes to chalazion formation, especially as a hordeolum resolves.

symptoms.symptom_name: Vigorous eye rubbing / manipulation of the lesion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Rubbing seeds and exacerbates the follicular infection and is the behaviour targeted by do-not-squeeze advice.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: A tender pointing pustule at a lash follicle, foreign-body sensation and redness confined to the lid margin together constitute the classic external hordeolum pattern and simultaneously localise the process away from the diffuse periorbital involvement of cellulitis.

Normal vision, no painful eye movements or diplopia, and being afebrile together are the patient-reportable exclusion set for orbital cellulitis — the discriminators that make remote conservative management safe.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Conjunctival/pustule swab for microscopy and culture
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: A swab is not routinely indicated for a simple external hordeolum; a Staphylococcus aureus culture only assists in refractory or atypical cases and is not needed acutely. Recommending it here is over-investigation.
